content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 23, 1990


Marilyn Wiczynski
Acting Deputy City Attorney
City of Garden Grove
P. O. Box 3070
Garden Grove, CA  92642

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-162

Dear Ms. Wiczynski:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning the duties of two city councilmembers for the City of
Garden Grove under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

     1.  Does the ownership of his residence, which is located on
the street to be improved and which is between 80 and 920 feet
from the properties to be acquired (possibly through
condemnations) and used for a street improvement project (the
project) by the City of Garden Grove , disqualify Councilmember
Williams from voting on any or all of the condemnations or
acquisitions?

     2.  Does the ownership of his residence, which is
respectively between 370 and 560 feet from the properties to be
acquired (possibly through condemnations) and used for a street
improvement project (the project) by the City of Garden Grove,
disqualify Councilmember Kessler from voting on any or all of the
condemnations or acquisitions?


___________________________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. A-90-162
Page 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. The ownership of his residence, which is on the street to
be improved and which is between 80 and 920 feet from the
properties to be acquired by the City of Garden Grove (possibly
through condemnations) and used for street improvement project,
would disqualify Councilmember Williams from voting on the
condemnations or acquisitions if the decision will have any
financial effect upon his property interest unless (1) all the
properties that are roughly the same distance away from the
proposed project are similarly affected and the property owners
constitute a significant segment of the population or (2) the
effect on the remainder of the population will be substantially
the same as the effect on him.

2. The ownership of his residence, which is respectively
between 370 and 560 feet from the properties to be acquired by the
City of Garden Grove (possibly through condemnations) and used for
a street improvement project, would disqualify Councilmember
Kessler from voting on the condemnations or acquisitions if those
decisions would affect the value of his property by $10,000 or
more affect the rental value of his property by $1,200 per year
or more unless (1) all the properties that are roughly the same
distance away from the proposed project are similarly affected and
the property owners constitute a significant segment of the
population, or (2) the effect on the remainder of the population
will be substantially the same as the effect on him.

FACTS

Project No. 735 encompasses Chapman Avenue between Euclid
Street and Ninth Street, a distance of .6 miles, all within the
City of Garden Grove. Chapman Avenue is a main arterial street
within the City of Garden Grove. Chapman Avenue runs east and
west and is being widened from the existing width of 80 feet to
100 feet to accommodate more traffic.

The project was approved for funding from the Orange County
Transportation Commission in the fiscal year 1988-89. It includes
new paving, street widening, and street lights.

A vote was taken on the awarding of a contract involving
Project No. 735 at the council meeting of February 20, 1990.

Two councilmembers, Frank Kessler and J. Tilman Williams,
abstained from voting on the contract award because of the
location of their individual residences.

The project calls for the acquisitions of 20 feet of frontage
from 12 parcels on Chapman Avenue. This will take place by
condemnation or acquisition in the future and will need council
approval.

The Garden Grove City Council consists of four councilmembers
and a mayor, elected at large.

Our File No. A-90-162
Page 3


Councilmember J. Tilman Williams, who lives at 11241 Chapman
Avenue, which is a corner lot. The property is between 80 and 920
feet from the properties to be acquired.

Councilmember Frank Kessler lives on a private road at 11352
Bixler Drive. It has no direct access to Chapman Avenue. The
properties fronting Chapman Avenue which will be affected by the
street widening project are between 370 feet and 560 feet from
Councilmember Kessler's home. Councilmember Kessler's residence
is 285 feet from the north curb of Chapman Avenue at its nearest
point.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. City councilmembers are public
officials. (Section 82048.)

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

Section 87103(b).

According to the information you have provided, each of the
two councilmen owns a residence on one parcel of land. It is
assumed for purposes of this analysis that the value of each
property is greater than $1,000 and that neither is a source of
rental income.

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision only if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
his property or source of income.

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198,
copy enclosed.)

Our File No. A-90-162
Page 4


Materiality of the Financial Effect of the Decision on
Councilmember Williams' Interest

The materiality of the financial effect of the decision must
also be determined. Since the decision to acquire the property
for road improvement purposes, subdivision (a)(2) of Regulation
18702.3 (copy enclosed) would apply to determine whether the
effect of the decision is material. That subdivision provides
that the effect of the decision is material if:

     The decision involves construction of, or
improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm
drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will
receive new or substantially improved services.

Regulation 18702.3(a)(2).

     Even though the decision does not specifically call for
improvement of roads adjacent to Councilmember Williams' property,
the purpose of the acquisition is clearly for the improvement of
Chapman Avenue. Improvement of that road, which at one point runs
adjacent to his property, will result in substantially improved
service to his property.

     The fact that Councilmember Williams' property is less than
300 feet from the project area also gives rise to a somewhat
different analysis. If the property in which the official has an
interest is within 300 feet of the property which is the subject
of the decision, the effect on the official's economic interest is
considered material "unless the decision will have no effect upon
the official's real property interest." (Regulation
18702.3(a)(1).) It would seem that improvements to Chapman Avenue
would affect the value of Councilmember Williams' property.

     In light of the foregoing, it appears that Councilmember
Williams must disqualify himself from participation in the subject
decision unless (1) all the properties that are roughly the same
distance away from the proposed project are similarly affected and
the property owners constitute a significant segment of the
population or (2) the effect on the remainder of the population
will be substantially the same as the effect on him (discussed
below under the heading, "Public Generally").


Materiality of the Effect of the Decision on Councilmember
Kessler's Interest

     The residence of Councilmember Kessler is within 370 feet of
the project area at its nearest point. Subdivision (a) of
Regulation 18702.3 provides guidelines as to whether the effect of
a decision on the real property interest of a public official
where the property is outside a radius of 300 feet, yet within a
radius of 2,500 feet, is material. In these circumstances, the

Our File No. A-90-162
Page 5

effect of the decision is material if the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable effect of:

(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an inter-
est; or

(B) Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.

Regulation 18702.3(a)(3) (copy
enclosed).

Thus, Councilmember Kessler must disqualify himself from
participating in any decision concerning the project that could
reasonably increase or decrease the fair market value of his
property by $10,000 or more, or the rental value of his property
by at least $1,000 in a 12-month period (Regulation
18702.3(a)(3)(A)) unless (1) all the properties that are roughly
the same distance away from the proposed project are similarly
affected and the property owners constitute a significant segment
of the population or (2) the effect on the remainder of the
population will be substantially the same as the effect on him
(discussed below under the heading, "Public Generally").

We cannot determine the magnitude of the financial effect on
Councilmember Kessler's property that will be caused by the
decisions on the project. We must leave this factual determination
of materiality to you within the guidelines provided by Regulation
18702.3. However, Regulation 18702.3(d) does set forth factors
that you must consider in determining whether the decisions will
have a material financial effect on the value of an official's
real property.² You must consider the following:

1. The proximity of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the magnitude of the
proposed project or change in use in relationship
to the property in which the official has an inter-
est;

2. Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the property;

3. In addition to the foregoing, in the case
of residential property, whether it is reasonably

___________
2 The factors to be considered are not limited to the factors
specified in Regulation 18702.3(d).

Our File No. A-90-162
Page 6

foreseeable that the decision will result in a
change to the character of the neighborhood
including, but not limited to, the effect on
traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the
neighborhood.

Regulation 18702.3(d).

For example, even though the property that is the subject of
the decision may be close to Councillmember Kessler's property, if
the magnitude of the proposed project or change in use in
relationship to his property is relatively minor, the impact
outside the project area may also be minor. In contrast, where a
decision is of substantial magnitude or involves a drastic change
in use, the fact that his property is distant may mitigate against
substantial effects on his property. Finally, since Councillmember
Kessler's property is currently used for residential purposes, he
must also consider any effect on the character of the neighborhood
in which his property is located with respect to traffic, view,
privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar
traits of the neighborhood. All these facts should be
considered in determining the financial effect the decision will
have on his property.

Public Generally

Even if it is determined that a decision will foreseeably and
materially affect real property in which the official has an
interest, the official may participate in the decision if the
effect on his property will be substantially the same as the
effect on the public generally. (Regulation 18703, copy
enclosed.)

For this "public generally" exception to apply, a decision
must affect the official's interests in substantially the same
manner as it would affect a significant segment of the residents
and persons doing business in the City of Garden Grove.
(Regulation 18703, copy enclosed; In re Owen, supra.) Where the
official's property is between 300 and 2,500 feet of the property
that is the subject of the decisions, he must show that all the
properties that are roughly the same distance away from the
proposed project are similarly affected and that these property
owners constitute a significant segment of the population, or that
the effect on the remainder of the population will be
substantially the same as the effect on him. (Cosgrove Advice
Letter, No. I-89-178, copy enclosed.)

Because the results of the test will vary depending on the
specific facts of the decision, we cannot provide you with a
definitive conclusion as to whether the exception would apply.
Instead, we have enclosed various letters and opinions that deal
with the exception and leave the factual determination as to its
applicability to you. (In re Brown (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 19; In re

Our File No. A-90-162
Page 7


Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops 62; In re Legan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1;
Scher Advice Letter No. A-88-479; Cosgrove Advice Letter No.
A-89-120, copies enclosed.)

     Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Kathryn E. Donovan
                                        General Counsel

                                        By: Joseph Garcia
                                            Counsel, Legal Division
KED:JG:aa

Enclosures

the street to be improved and which is between 80 and 920 feet
from the properties to be acguired (possibLy through
condemnations) and used for a street improvement project (the
project) by the City of Garden Grove , disqualify Councilmember
wittians from votinq on any or all of the condemnations or
acquis itions?

disqualify Councilnember Kessler from votinq on any or a1l- of the
condemnations or acquisitions?

be improved and which is between 80 and 920 feet from the
properties to be acquired by the City of carden Grove (possibly

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission April 23, 1990 Marilyn Wiczynski Acting Deputy City Attorney City of Garden Grove P. O. Box 3070 Garden Grove, CA 92642 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-90-162 Dear Ms. Wiczynski: This is in response to your letter requesting advice concerning the duties of two city councilmembers for the City of Garden Grove under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTIONS 1. Does the ownership of his residence, which is located on the street to be improved and which is between 80 and 920 feet from the properties to be acquired (possibly through condemnations) and used for a street improvement project (the project) by the City of Garden Grove , disqualify Councilmember Williams from voting on any or all of the condemnations or acquisitions? 2. Does the ownership of his residence, which is respectively between 370 and 560 feet from the properties to be acquired (possibly through condemnations) and used for a street improvement project (the project) by the City of Garden Grove, disqualify Councilmember Kessler from voting on any or all of the condemnations or acquisitions? ___________________________ 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 Our File No. A-90-162 Page 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. The ownership of his residence, which is on the street to be improved and which is between 80 and 920 feet from the properties to be acquired by the City of Garden Grove (possibly through condemnations) and used for street improvement project, would disqualify Councilmember Williams from voting on the condemnations or acquisitions if the decision will have any financial effect upon his property interest unless (1) all the properties that are roughly the same distance away from the proposed project are similarly affected and the property owners constitute a significant segment of the population or (2) the effect on the remainder of the population will be substantially the same as the effect on him. 2. The ownership of his residence, which is respectively between 370 and 560 feet from the properties to be acquired by the City of Garden Grove (possibly through condemnations) and used for a street improvement project, would disqualify Councilmember Kessler from voting on the condemnations or acquisitions if those decisions would affect the value of his property by $10,000 or more affect the rental value of his property by $1,200 per year or more unless (1) all the properties that are roughly the same distance away from the proposed project are similarly affected and the property owners constitute a significant segment of the population, or (2) the effect on the remainder of the population will be substantially the same as the effect on him. FACTS Project No. 735
